Bioprocessing Advances: Modern Fermentation Techniques
------------------------------------------------------

Bioprocessing is like a modern-day symphony.

Recent advances in bioprocessing have transformed fermentation techniques into pivotal components of sustainable production.

These innovations enable the cultivation of microorganisms to efficiently produce high-value compounds. Imagine harnessing the power of nature to create more affordable, and environmentally friendly products through advanced bioprocessing techniques.

Innovation leads to remarkable outcomes.

Introduction to Modern Fermentation
-----------------------------------

Modern fermentation is revolutionizing bioprocessing through advanced techniques and cutting-edge technology to redefine sustainable production.

Historically rooted in age-old practices, fermentation has evolved into an essential pillar of modern bioprocessing. Advances in scientific understanding have transformed traditional methods into highly efficient processes that unlock new possibilities across industries, from pharmaceuticals to food and energy.

Today, modern fermentation utilizes state-of-the-art bioreactors and precise microbial engineering, allowing us to optimize yield and quality. By harnessing genetically engineered microorganisms, the process can be tailored to produce specific compounds with remarkable efficiency.

The implications of these modern techniques are vast, offering the potential to significantly reduce environmental impact while drastically lowering production costs. This synergy of nature and technology promises a future where bioprocessing not only meets the demands of today but also pioneers a sustainable tomorrow.

As we venture further into this exciting realm, modern fermentation continues to shape the landscape of bioprocessing.

Innovations in Bioprocessing
----------------------------

Recent strides in bioprocessing have unveiled a plethora of cutting-edge techniques, fuelled by relentless innovation, a commitment to sustainability, and a global ambition to redefine production frameworks.

Advanced digitization paves the way for real-time monitoring and adaptive bioprocessing, enhancing overall efficiency.

Moreover, novel downstream processing techniques are refining product purity, thereby maximizing value (advancing sustainability and resource efficiency).

These breakthroughs lead to more resilient processes, empowering industries to rapidly adapt and evolve amidst changing global demands.

Unprecedented approaches in synthetic biology and bioprocessing are paving pathways into new therapeutic frontiers, unlocking waves of possibilities for disease management and prevention while fostering economic growth.

Thus, the era of innovation in bioprocessing is not just a possibility; it is happening now. Equipped with these advanced methodologies, industries can confidently stride into a future brimming with potential.

Fermentation Technology Evolution
---------------------------------

Throughout history, fermentation has been a cornerstone of bioprocessing, shaping countless industries and cultures. Modern innovations have transformed this ancient art into a sophisticated science, integral to industrial advancement.

In recent years, breakthroughs in fermentation technology have surged, fueled by interdisciplinary collaboration and technological progress. These developments are catalysts for heightened efficiency, scalability, and sustainability across biotechnological applications, solidifying fermentation’s pivotal role.

Today, the integration of automation and real-time analytics has redefined fermentation processes. These advancements enable precise control environments and optimize product yield, significantly enhancing the efficiency of microbial and cell cultures. The advent of advanced sensors and AI-driven analytics ensures proactive decision-making capabilities.

Companies are increasingly leveraging genetic engineering to create tailored microbial hosts, enhancing process efficiency and product specificity. These innovations underscore a shift towards more predictable and robust fermentation systems, revolutionizing industries from pharmaceuticals to food production.

As the journey of fermentation technology continues to unfold, its potential seems boundless. Bright opportunities lie ahead, inspiring confidence in the future of bioprocessing.

Industrial Scale Fermentation
-----------------------------

Industrial scale fermentation is the heart of modern bioprocessing, driving efficiency in numerous industries. At such scales, enormous quantities of microbial cultures are processed to capitalize on their biochemical capabilities, from producing biofuels to pharmaceuticals.

In recent years, innovation has surged, enabling more consistent monitoring and control during large-scale fermentations. Emerging technologies such as sophisticated sensors and AI-driven systems have vastly improved oversight, ensuring optimal conditions for microbial growth and activity.

Remarkably, these advancements are complemented by the development of novel bioreactors that enhance mass transfer and energy efficiency. This ensures that larger batches can be produced without compromising quality, aligning with sustainability goals and economic feasibility.

Moreover, the integration of real-time data analytics is revolutionizing industrial fermentation by enabling predictive maintenance and minimizing downtime. This proactive approach is paramount in maintaining the high productivity necessary for industries dependent on fermentation.

With these advancements, the future of industrial scale fermentation holds immense promise, paving the way for unprecedented bioprocessing opportunities. Bright horizons beckon across the realm of biotechnology.

Types of Fermentation Processes
-------------------------------

In the vibrant field of bioprocessing, fermentation processes are diverse and intricately tailored to specific needs, including batch, fed-batch, and continuous formats. Each style, whether anaerobic or aerobic, is a testament to how precision and adaptability can unlock the true potential of various organisms to produce valuable compounds.

These distinct fermentation techniques seamlessly weave innovation with tradition, driving sustainable progress and the commercial scalability of bioprocessing endeavors.

### Aerobic Fermentation Methods

[Aerobic fermentation](https://ritaibioreactor.com/aerobic-fermentation/) is a breath of fresh air—literally—transforming bioprocessing with its oxygen-rich undertones.

> Aerobic processes not only boost efficiency but are crucial for producing essential vitamins and antibiotics.

Leveraging oxygen as a catalyst, aerobic fermentation methods push the frontiers of productivity, enabling the efficient transformation of substrates into critical biochemical products. This method underscores the symbiotic power of biology and technology.

By harmonizing nature’s capabilities with human ingenuity, aerobic fermentation presents a robust framework for scaling up the production of a myriad of bioproducts. It promises a future where efficiency intertwines with sustainability.

### Anaerobic Fermentation Techniques

Anaerobic fermentation techniques are the silent champions of bioprocessing, driving innovation without the need for oxygen.

*   **Butyric Acid Fermentation**: Transforms carbohydrates into butyric acid, ideal for biofuel production.
*   **Lactic Acid Fermentation**: Converts glucose into lactic acid, vital for food and pharmaceutical industries.
*   **Ethanolic Fermentation**: Ferments sugars to create ethanol, a cornerstone of the biofuel sector.
*   **Methanogenesis**: Produces methane from organic matter, a key player in sustainable energy solutions.

Anaerobic fermentation is a cornerstone of circular economies, transforming waste into wealth efficiently.

By focusing on energy conservation and productivity, these techniques foster environmental stewardship while driving industrial innovation forward.

Advances in Bioreactor Design
-----------------------------

[Bioreactor design](https://ritaibioreactor.com/bioreactor-design/) continues to evolve, embracing new opportunities for enhancing bioprocessing efficiency and innovation. Cutting-edge technologies now integrate smarter systems for more dynamic operations.

Flexible and adaptable bioreactor platforms offer newfound agility in adjusting process parameters.

These advances allow for personalized creation of optimal environments tailored for specific microorganisms, fostering a surge in productivity, sustainability, and biosafety that is truly transformative.

The modern bioreactor embodies the future of bioprocessing, carving a path where versatility and precision redefine the landscape of bio-based manufacturing. By acting as the “heart” of bioengineering and with continued innovation and optimization, it promises unprecedented advancements.

Role of Biocatalysts
--------------------

Biocatalysts, essential agents in modern bioprocessing, profoundly enhance the efficiency and sustainability of fermentation. Their sophisticated mechanisms drive trust in their transformative potential.

Biocatalysts drastically reduce the need for harsh chemicals in bioprocessing systems.

Innovatively, they enable enzyme-driven reactions that are both highly specific and environmentally benign.

These catalysts shine in diverse applications from pharmaceuticals to biofuels, underpinning a greener future.

With their precision, biocatalysts enhance process efficiency, cut costs, and foster cleaner technologies, igniting a new era of sustainable industrial processes. They embody innovation.

Indeed, the essence of biocatalysts lies in their capacity to streamline reactions. Their boundless potential is paving the way for ever-more efficient, sustainable bioprocessing solutions.

Applications in Food Industry
-----------------------------

In the realm of bioprocessing, modern fermentation techniques are revolutionizing the food industry.

Fermentation’s profound impact is evidenced by enhanced nutritional profiles and preservation.

These methods offer innovative solutions to creating healthier food products, expanding their role as a beacon of sustainable practices within the industry. From crafting rich flavors to preserving global culinary traditions, fermentation continues to inspire advancement, sustainability, and exploration of new horizons.

The adoption of these cutting-edge techniques in food production raises industry standards to a new pinnacle of quality and innovation. As consumers demand more sustainable and health-conscious choices, the application of fermentation in food bioprocessing becomes indispensable. In this vibrant field, bioprocessing acts as a “master key” to unlocking the future of food innovation.

Pharmaceutical Applications
---------------------------

Bioprocessing in the pharmaceutical sector holds immense promise, revolutionizing the production of essential medicines. Through advanced fermentation techniques, pharmaceutical bioprocessing enhances efficiency and precision, ensuring unparalleled quality and consistency in drug manufacturing.

In 2016, bioprocessing breakthroughs, including various fermentation methodologies, reshaped how biologics and small molecules are produced. By leveraging living cells and enzymes, these advances accelerate drug development, reduce costs, and improve safety and efficacy, catalyzing unprecedented growth in pharmaceutical innovation.

Today, it’s no surprise that pharma’s reliance on bioprocessing grows as a cornerstone of creating life-saving therapies. Bioprocess technology has transformed the production capacities of vaccines and other pharmaceuticals that address global health challenges, fostering better patient outcomes worldwide.

Moreover, the evolution of these methods has paved the way for personalized medicine’s exciting potential. With the rise of cell therapies, gene therapies, and precise biologics, bioprocessing ensures that these transformative treatments reach patients with both unparalleled speed and reliability.

Pharmaceutical bioprocessing is driving a brighter future, transforming potential into tangible results, and promising boundless advancements.

Environmental Impact of Fermentation
------------------------------------

Fermentation, as a bioprocessing technology, plays a pivotal role in reducing environmental footprints. By harnessing natural processes, it effectively curtails the reliance on fossil fuels, mitigating associated emissions.

These sustainability benefits stem from the cellular activities central to fermentation.

Bioprocessing, especially through modern fermentation techniques, maximizes renewable resource use (microbial cultures and biomass) while minimizing waste.

Recent innovations aim to enhance fermentation’s environmental efficiency by optimizing resource utilization, promoting closed-loop systems.

This shift towards bioprocessing not only significantly cuts down on hazardous waste but also demonstrates the remarkable potential for biologically-driven clean solutions that nurture the earth and support global sustainability.

Ultimately, the goal is clear: leverage fermentation’s innate capabilities to rejuvenate the planet. Bioprocessing represents a future where ecological harmony and industrial efficiency coexist.

Challenges in Modern Bioprocessing
----------------------------------

While the advances in bioprocessing are remarkable, the path to perfection isn’t devoid of obstacles.

In 2023, researchers identified, a significant challenge being the scalability of bioprocessing technologies, which often lags behind the rapid advancements in lab-scale processes.

Moreover, it’s not just about finding solutions; there’s also the rigorous task of integrating new bioprocessing models into existing industrial frameworks, ensuring smooth transitions and optimal performance.

Addressing these challenges requires the dedication to investing in research, fostering cross-disciplinary collaboration, and embracing innovative technologies that align with the principles of Industry 4.0, such as digital twins and AI-powered analytics.

Despite the hurdles, optimism prevails as the [bioprocessing community](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/manufacturing-processes/bioprocessing/bioprocessing-everything-need-know) continues to strive for groundbreaking solutions with unwavering determination.

Future Trends in Fermentation Technology
----------------------------------------

The landscape of fermentation is rapidly evolving.

Emerging trends have sparked excitement and new possibilities. The future looks promising as we embrace the seamless integration of advanced fermentation techniques, notably in the production of sustainable bioproducts. Innovations, such as the use of synthetic biology and precision fermentation, pave the way for efficient pathways and optimal yields.

AI-driven fermentation promises revolutionary breakthroughs.

These upcoming advancements aim to – not only address today’s challenges but predict tomorrow’s needs, enabling producers to pinpoint exact fermentation conditions for any desired product.

Today’s fermentative innovations are tomorrow’s industrial mainstays, unlocking unprecedented opportunities to revolutionize industries, ranging from pharmaceuticals to biofuels, and reshaping our approach to sustainability. Our collective vision for the future of fermentation technology in 2023 and beyond remains both exhilarating and hopeful.

==========================================================================================================================================================

Highlights
----------

*   •Flow field difference across different scales is a key aspect that influence the scale-up process. 
*   •Strain physiological response to heterogeneous environment makes the scale-up more complicated. 
*   •Rational scale-up strategies depend on both flow field and physiological response. 

Abstract
--------

The production capability of a fermentation process is predominately determined by individual strains, which ultimately affected ultimately by interactions between the scale-dependent flow field developed within [bioreactors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/biochemical-reactor "Learn more about bioreactors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the physiological response of these strains. Interpreting these complicated interactions is key for better understanding the scale-up of the fermentation process. We review these two aspects and address progress in strategies for scaling up fermentation processes. A perspective on how to incorporate the multiomics big data into the scale-up strategy is presented to improve the design and operation of industrial fermentation processes.

Introduction
------------

Industrial fermentation utilizes microorganisms to produce various products, including antibiotics [1], organic acids [2], pharmaceutical proteins [3], and industrial enzymes [4], _etc_. It is estimated that the biotechnology products cover one-third of the worldwide market, with a value of more than 300 million USD [5]. Industrial fermentation is advantageous because it involves relatively moderate pressure and temperature, and is more sustainable in terms of the use of natural resources, compared to chemical reaction processes. Thus, it has attracted considerable interest and promoted the emergence of a bio-economy. Furthermore, with the progress in metabolic engineering and synthetic biology, it is relatively easier to obtain engineered cell factories with improved product yield, even producing new products [6], [7]. High-throughput screening techniques have been developing at great speed. However, process development remains at a lag (3–10 years), and the cost of scaling up the process from lab-scale to industrial production is generally on the order of 100 million USD to 1 billion USD [8].

Scaling up newly developed lab-scale fermentation processes is hampered by a poor understanding of the principles that govern the scale-up requirements [8], [9]. Empirical approaches based on principle of similarity and dimensional analysis for scale-up are always implemented by the general principle of maintaining specific parameters as constants during the scale-up (_e_._g_., specific power consumption rate, oxygen transfer coefficient, impeller tip speed, mixing time, _etc_.) [10]. It has been noticed that scaling up a fermentation process from a shake flask to an industrial fermenter consists of challenges [11], [12], as non-homogeneous conditions in industrial fermenters may cause negative effects on strains. Even though it is common to keep specific parameters constant during scale-up in practice, it is necessary to find the critical influential parameters, and it is impossible to keep those properties (such as flow dynamics or fluid kinetics) similar or simultaneous at different scales [11]. The interaction of two aspects affecting the scale-up process should be studied carefully for successful scaling up. One aspect is the flow field condition in bioreactors, which is determined by many parameters, including geometric configurations, fluid rheology and rotation speed. The other aspect is the physiological response of microbial cells, which is determined by their genetic background (genome) and complicated metabolism regulation systems. We analyzed the relationship between the flow field and the physiology of cells in both nature and in a bioreactor, as summarized in Fig. 1.

This review is mainly organized to cover two aspects. First, we discuss flow field issues when scaling up, in which mixing efficiency, mass transfer coefficient and shear forces and their influence on fermentation are discussed. Following that, the cell physiological response to the flow field is reviewed, as are _in vivo_ kinetic studies with integration to the flow in bioreactors. This is with a view to understanding the scale-up process. Finally, a short perspective is given on how multiomics data can be used to better understand the scale-up process and what can be discovered with the help of big data on bioprocesses.

Physiological Responses of Microbial Cells to Surrounding Environment
---------------------------------------------------------------------

One of the key challenges for fermentation scale up is the flow field difference between lab-scale and industrial-scale bioreactors. The constraint of specific power input in large-scale bioreactors always results in heterogeneous environments in the large bioreactors. The relevant properties of the strain cultured in the fluctuating environment are not studied in the lab-scale process development stage because only homogeneous distributed nutrients is formed in lab-scale bioreactor.

Abstract

The article discusses the potential of precision fermentation for sustainable protein production and the key challenges of scaling it up. As the global demand for sustainable and ethical food sources intensifies, precision fermentation has emerged as a promising technology for producing alternative proteins. This innovative process, which uses microbial hosts to create proteins identical to those found in animal products, offers a way to reduce the environmental footprint of food production while addressing ethical concerns associated with traditional livestock. However, despite its potential, precision fermentation faces significant challenges when scaling up from laboratory to commercial production. These challenges, which we defined as the "ghost of scale-up," include technological hurdles, economic barriers, regulatory complexities, and the need for environmental and social sustainability.

# The Promise and Potential of Precision Fermentation

Precision fermentation is based on the genetic modification of microorganisms, such as bacteria, yeast, or fungi, to produce specific proteins or other valuable molecules. This technology, well-known in pharmaceutical production, has shown great promise in creating ingredients for food products, such as dairy proteins, animal-free. The process can theoretically produce these proteins at a fraction of the environmental cost associated with conventional farming, offering a solution to the pressing need for sustainable protein sources.

# Technological and Economic Challenges

Scaling up precision fermentation is not a straightforward task. At the laboratory scale, conditions can be meticulously controlled to optimize the growth and productivity of microbial cultures. However, replicating these conditions on an industrial scale is much more complex. One of the key technological challenges is the optimization of fermentation processes to achieve high yields and consistent product quality at a larger scale. This involves refining the genetic modifications of microorganisms, as well as developing bioreactors that can maintain the necessary conditions for microbial growth and protein expression, defining the correct downstream pathway to obtain product with required specifications (i.e. purity, appearance) on a massive scale.

In addition to technological hurdles, economic challenges can be scary. The capital expenditure required to build and operate large-scale precision fermentation facilities is substantial. These facilities require significant investment in fermentation infrastructure and downstream processing equipment. For many start-ups as well as for smaller companies, securing the necessary funding can be a formidable barrier. Moreover, the cost of raw materials, energy, and labor can make the production of precision-fermented proteins more expensive than traditional animal proteins, at least in the short term. Achieving economies of scale and optimizing supply chains will be critical to making these alternative proteins economically viable.

![](/digital/agrofood_industry_hi_tech_35_3_2024/precision_fermentation_-_the_ghost_of_scale-up_in_precision_fermentation/894582/immagine_tank.800_0_1.jpg)

# ​​​​​​​Approach to Bioprocess Design

Scaling up is not a linear process. A specific approach is required to design a process suitable for commercial-scale production. Process design should start early, right after proof-of-concept, accompanied by a techno-economic analysis to optimize design and reduce the risks and costs associated with scaling up.

**Key Considerations in Process Design**

# Comparison of Scale-Up Approaches

A classic approach to scale up can be simplified as follows:

An agile, integrated approach, that our team promotes, is the following:

This agile approach provides a more effective strategy for scaling up processes by integrating multiple areas of expertise early in the process and fostering continuous collaboration. Unlike traditional methods that often delay industrial optimization, this approach anticipates process design right from the proof-of-concept stage, allowing for early identification and resolution of potential bottlenecks. By involving both lab and engineering teams from the outset, key factors such as bioreactor configuration, raw material selection, and process conditions can be optimized in tandem, streamlining the scale-up.

A crucial element of this agile strategy is continuous strain engineering, where microbial strains are iteratively refined to improve yield, robustness, and consistency. This allows for faster adaptations to changing conditions, enabling microbial hosts to achieve higher production efficiencies at every step of the scale-up. Simultaneously, strain improvements are assessed in real time for their impact on productivity, cost, and scalability, ensuring that the organism and process are both optimized before reaching industrial scale.

Additionally, the agile approach follows a clear criterion for increasing production volume, based on achieving specific production efficiencies and reducing production costs. Rather than scaling up in a linear fashion, decisions about increasing capacity are driven by measurable performance indicators such as yield, energy use, and resource consumption. By targeting these efficiencies early, this method ensures that volume expansion occurs only when the cost per unit of product is minimized, moving faster towards the Target Price.

In summary, this agile and integrated approach not only accelerates the scale-up process but also significantly reduces associated costs and risks through early process design, continuous strain optimization, and a production scale-up criterion based on efficiency and cost reduction.

# Navigating Regulatory Hurdles

Regulatory approval is another significant challenge for scaling up precision fermentation. In order to bring precision-fermented products to market, companies must navigate complex and varied regulatory landscapes. In regions like the United States and Europe, regulatory bodies such as the FDA and EFSA require rigorous safety and nutritional assessments to ensure that these products are safe for human consumption. This process can be time-consuming and costly, especially for novel products that involve genetically modified organisms (GMOs).

Moreover, companies must implement robust quality control measures to prevent contamination and ensure that their products meet the highest safety standards.

# Environmental and Social Sustainability

Scaling up precision fermentation production introduces new sustainability challenges. The process requires significant water and energy inputs, and waste management becomes increasingly important as production scales up. Companies must work to minimize the environmental impact of their operations by optimizing resource use, reducing waste and reusing byproducts, and implementing sustainable practices throughout the supply chain.

Social challenges, including consumer acceptance and cultural adaptation, also play a crucial role in the success of precision fermentation. Consumers may be skeptical of foods produced through biotechnological processes, particularly those involving GMOs. Building consumer trust through transparent communication, education, and engagement is essential. Additionally, precision-fermented products must be tailored to fit into diverse cultural and dietary contexts to achieve widespread adoption.

![](/digital/agrofood_industry_hi_tech_35_3_2024/precision_fermentation_-_the_ghost_of_scale-up_in_precision_fermentation/894581/shutter-stock_2153855559.800_0_1.jpg)

# ​​​​​​​Pathways to Overcoming the “Ghost” of Scale-Up

Despite these challenges, the potential of precision fermentation is too significant to ignore. Overcoming the ghost of scale-up will require a multifaceted approach that includes continued investment in research and development, strategic partnerships, and collaboration across the industry. Innovations in genetic engineering, fermentation technology, and downstream processing are essential to improving the efficiency and scalability of precision fermentation.

Furthermore, building effective regulatory frameworks and engaging with regulators early in the development process can help companies navigate the complex approval processes. By working together, companies, governments, and research institutions can create a supportive environment for the growth of the precision fermentation industry.

Finally, sustainability must be a core focus. Companies must conduct comprehensive life cycle assessments to understand and mitigate the environmental impact of their products. By committing to sustainable practices and addressing social equity, the precision fermentation industry can ensure that it not only scales up successfully but also contributes to a more sustainable and equitable global food system.

# Conclusion

The journey from laboratory-scale precision fermentation to large-scale commercial production is loaded with challenges, but it is also filled with opportunities. By addressing the technological, economic, regulatory, and sustainability challenges—and by carefully selecting the appropriate scale-up approach—the precision fermentation industry can overcome the ghost of scale-up and unlock its full potential. With continued innovation and collaboration, precision fermentation can play a pivotal role in shaping the future of food, providing sustainable, nutritious, and widely accepted alternatives to conventional animal proteins.

Abstract
--------

In the era of sustainable development, the use of cell factories to produce various compounds by fermentation has attracted extensive attention; however, industrial fermentation requires not only efficient production strains, but also suitable extracellular conditions and medium components, as well as scaling-up. In this regard, the use of biological models has received much attention, and this review will provide guidance for the rapid selection of biological models. This paper first introduces two mechanistic modeling methods, kinetic modeling and constraint-based modeling (CBM), and generalizes their applications in practice. Next, we review data-driven modeling based on machine learning (ML), and highlight the application scope of different learning algorithms. The combined use of ML and CBM for constructing hybrid models is further discussed. At the end, we also discuss the recent strategies for predicting bioreactor scale-up and culture behavior through a combination of biological models and computational fluid dynamics (CFD) models.

**Keywords:** mechanistic modeling, data-driven, hybrid modeling, scale-up, computational fluid dynamics

1. Introduction
---------------

With the increasing consumption of fossil fuels and related environmental issues, there is an urgent need to find biological substitutes for traditional petrochemical products through green biological manufacturing. Over the decades, microorganisms have been used as ‘mini-factories’ in biomanufacturing to aid with the diversity of metabolic pathways and their accompanying ability to transform a wide range of renewable raw materials into value-added compounds by means of fermentation [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B1-bioengineering-09-00473),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B2-bioengineering-09-00473),[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B3-bioengineering-09-00473)]; however, the use of wild-type microorganisms in industrial production is usually affected by many factors, such as substrate and product toxicity [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B4-bioengineering-09-00473),[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B5-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Biologists are provided with many modern biotechnologies, such as genetic engineering and synthetic biology, to engineer more powerful mini-factories [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B6-bioengineering-09-00473),[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B7-bioengineering-09-00473),[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B8-bioengineering-09-00473),[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B9-bioengineering-09-00473),[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B10-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Successful examples include _Escherichia coli_, which produce insulin and carotene [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B11-bioengineering-09-00473),[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B12-bioengineering-09-00473)], _Saccharomyces cerevisiae_, which produce geraniol [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B13-bioengineering-09-00473)], _Yarrowia lipolytica_, which produce N-acetylneuraminic acid [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B14-bioengineering-09-00473)], _Bacillus subtilis_, which produce hyaluronic acid [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B15-bioengineering-09-00473)], and so on; however, the construction of mini-factories is not the end goal of biomanufacturing—biological fermentation and industrial production are the ultimate goal, which is not a simple task. The engineered strains may not perform well in the actual fermentation process due to the lack of suitable fermentation strategies; therefore, optimizing fermentation parameters (such as the medium composition and extracellular conditions) is a key factor in terms of running the mini-factories efficiently, which is crucial for the process of fermentation [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B16-bioengineering-09-00473),[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B17-bioengineering-09-00473),[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B18-bioengineering-09-00473)]. In practice, the rich insights generated by mathematical modeling can assist in the optimization of fermentation processes [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B19-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Mathematical models, as approximations of reality, can clearly represent fermentation processes whose intrinsic complexity exceeds intuitive understanding, thus providing indispensable insight into designing, controlling, and optimizing the process, as well as minimizing unnecessary experimentation [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B20-bioengineering-09-00473),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B21-bioengineering-09-00473)].

However, it is not easy to model the fermentation process because each cell in the bioreactor can be viewed as a subsystem of metabolic and signaling networks [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B22-bioengineering-09-00473),[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B23-bioengineering-09-00473)]. For fermentation problems, three modeling approaches are generally used: mechanistic modeling, data-driven modeling, and hybrid modeling [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B20-bioengineering-09-00473),[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B24-bioengineering-09-00473)]. The mechanistic modeling approaches derive the models from prior knowledge using equations that are notable and acknowledged. Mechanistic models can extract valuable information from the raw data, and they provide insight into the underlying mechanisms [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B25-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Kinetics and constraint-based modeling (CBM) are the two primary mechanistic approaches for analyzing microbial growth and metabolism [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B26-bioengineering-09-00473)]. In contrast, the data-driven approaches obtain a model by analyzing and fitting existing data. Data-driven models are also known as black-box models because they cannot provide information about the basic mechanisms without considering their internal structures and phenomena [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B27-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Machine learning (ML) is a commonly used data-driven approach. With the advancement of omics technology and various analytical techniques, the datasets available for fermentation process modeling are rapidly growing, and academics favor using ML to interpret large-scale datasets for deeper analysis and optimization [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B28-bioengineering-09-00473)]. As a result of such circumstances, hybrid modeling has come into being, which refers to the integration of mechanistic modeling and data-driven approaches. Recently, comprehensive reviews of hybrid models have been published, indicating that they are a promising prospect for this field [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B29-bioengineering-09-00473),[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B30-bioengineering-09-00473)].

In addition to the complexity of microbial metabolic behavior, fermentation systems also have complex hierarchical structures. These systems consist of microorganisms and a fermentation environment, and they are influenced by upstream and downstream operating conditions. Moreover, during industrialization, the expansion of the bioreactor volume and changes in shape lead to changes in the fermentation environment, which subsequently leads to the failure of fermentation strategies that are developed during the laboratory stage [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B31-bioengineering-09-00473)]; therefore, as biological fermentation shifts from laboratory to industrial production, we also need to introduce fermentation environmental changes into biological models to elucidate the effects of mixing and hydrodynamics [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B32-bioengineering-09-00473),[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B33-bioengineering-09-00473)]. This goal can be achieved by coupling biological models with computational fluid dynamics (CFD) models.

This paper gives an overview of different mathematical modeling methods and their applications in biological fermentation processes. We first introduce the basic forms of mechanistic models that can describe microbial metabolism using kinetic and CBM modeling methods and their applications in biological fermentation processes. Next, we discuss different approaches to building data-driven models using ML. The synergistic effect of CBM and ML is further discussed. In the end, we highlight the coupling of biological models with CFD models, which prompts the formation of model-based integrated tools to successfully predict bioreactor scale-up and culture behavior during model-assisted bioreactor operation design.

2. Methods and Applications of Mechanistic Modeling
---------------------------------------------------

Mechanistic modeling describes some, though not all, mechanisms of complex systems, enabling model parameters to be measured or inferred. Kinetic models are a kind of common mechanistic model, which usually reveal the dynamic changes between metabolites by means of kinetic laws expressed by ordinary differential equations (ODEs) [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B34-bioengineering-09-00473)]; however, for kinetic models, the mechanism of gene regulation underlying biological phenomena is usually only explained by kinetic parameters in model equations (e.g., enzyme constants and metabolite concentrations), rather than a mechanistic description of gene action [[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B35-bioengineering-09-00473)], and therefore, kinetic modeling is generally applied to fermentation problems in which the metabolic behavior is well understood. CBM is another mechanistic modeling approach. CBM studies the behavior of genome-wide systems to elucidate the relationship between genotypes, phenotypes, and environmental conditions [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B27-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Next, we will discuss these two mechanistic modeling methods and their application to fermentation problems in detail.

### 2.1. Application of Kinetic Modeling to Fermentation Processes

The derivation of kinetic models depends mainly upon the level of detail in which microbial growth and metabolic behaviors are described. From a macro perspective, the fermentation process in a bioreactor is a chemical process affected by physical and chemical environmental factors (such as temperature, pH, aeration, and substrate concentration). The transformation of substrate S to product P is catalyzed by bacterial pellets X, which may be seen as a box containing equally disseminated catalysts and chemicals. In this case, we do not need to pay attention to the internal structure of bacterial pellets; we only need to pay attention to the changes in substances and external environmental factors before and after catalysis. Thus, the kinetic models provide a macro perspective on the bioreactor dynamics without specifying compositional and structural details under reasonable assumptions. The kinetic models constructed in this case can also be called unstructured kinetic models. When the substrate concentration (S) is the only limiting element, the Monod model is established. Different unstructured kinetic models of fermentation processes are established with different descriptions of constraints and common kinetic equations, as shown in [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#bioengineering-09-00473-t001). In practice, biologists construct kinetic models by adjusting and combining different equations according to the actual situation. For instance, Zhang et al., established a new kinetic model based on Monod through parameter optimization, which not only described the relationship between products and by-products in the fermentation process of 1, 3-propylene glycol, but it also guided the optimization of the fermentation conditions and improved the yield [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B36-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Moreover, Garnier et al. developed an analytical solution that combined the Monod model with the Luedeking–Piret equation which can be used to simulate the batch fermentation process [[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B37-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Although these models address the least complex portrayal of cellular behavior, they can prove particularly valuable when quantifying the phenomenon of growth inhibition during early process development. On the other hand, unstructured kinetic models generally contain few parameters, which, from a computational perspective, is usually advantageous. At present, a significant portion of recent studies concern bioethanol production [[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B38-bioengineering-09-00473),[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B39-bioengineering-09-00473),[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B40-bioengineering-09-00473)], biosurfactant production [[41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B41-bioengineering-09-00473),[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B42-bioengineering-09-00473)], bacterial and fungal biomass production [[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B43-bioengineering-09-00473),[44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B44-bioengineering-09-00473)], as well as microalgal growth [[45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B45-bioengineering-09-00473),[46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B46-bioengineering-09-00473),[47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B47-bioengineering-09-00473)].

As opposed to unstructured kinetic models, structured kinetic models consider the physiological state of the cell and treat bacterial pellets as multicomponent entities with internal structures. Structure, in structured kinetic models, can be introduced by lumping the metabolites into distinct intracellular pools [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B25-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Wang et al. constructed a structured kinetic model that can be used to dynamically reflect changes in microalgal biomass by analyzing how carbon is allocated to different intracellular compartments under nitrogen-poor and nitrogen-rich extracellular conditions [[56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B56-bioengineering-09-00473)]. In a similar manner, Haringa et al. constructed a structured kinetic model describing the growth of _Penicillium_ _chrysogenum_, as well as product formation, by grouping the most important intracellular metabolites into five pools and four intracellular enzyme pools [[57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B57-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Their research also proved that the structured models have a higher accuracy and wider application scope than the unstructured models. We believe that our improved understanding of the behavior of microbial cells, along with advances in various omics techniques, will facilitate the further development of structured kinetic modeling.

### 2.2. Application of Constraint-Based Modeling to Fermentation Processes

CBM is another important mechanistic modeling method that considers the underlying metabolic mechanisms of microorganisms. CBM can be used to analyze the dynamic characteristics of specific target metabolites, and then implement fermentation strategy optimization from the micro-level, which can enhance the diversity and pertinence of optimization target design [[58](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B58-bioengineering-09-00473)]. To build a CBM model, an in-depth understanding of metabolism is first required. Metabolism represents all the biochemical reactions in cells. These reactions form a coordinated set of metabolic pathways that convert substrates into the metabolites required for the physiological activities of the cells at a rate known as metabolic flux. In addition to being a basic determinant of a cellular metabolic state and physiology, metabolic flux is also the key parameter in quantifying metabolic networks [[59](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B59-bioengineering-09-00473)]. CBM plays an important role in the metabolic modeling of quantitative metabolic flux [[60](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B60-bioengineering-09-00473),[61](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B61-bioengineering-09-00473),[62](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B62-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Metabolic flux analysis (MFA) and Flux balance analysis (FBA) are the two primary CBM strategies for quantifying metabolic flux ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#bioengineering-09-00473-f001)), which can be implemented by COnstraint-Based Reconstruction and Analysis (COBRApy) in Python [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B26-bioengineering-09-00473),[63](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B63-bioengineering-09-00473)].

#### 2.2.1. Flux Balance Analysis

Flux balance analysis (FBA) is a mathematical metabolic flux technique that analyzes the flux of metabolites through a detailed metabolic network (e.g., genomic scale) [[64](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B64-bioengineering-09-00473)]. One of the earliest applications of FBA was used to calculate the given network model and substrate to obtain their maximum theoretical yields of metabolic products ([Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#bioengineering-09-00473-t002)) [[64](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B64-bioengineering-09-00473),[65](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B65-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Papoutsakis et al. first demonstrated this in 1984 by describing the interrelationship between various products and biomass in butyrate fermentation using FBA, accurately predicting the theoretical yield of the several fermentation products of butyrate bacteria [[66](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B66-bioengineering-09-00473)]. The same method can be utilized to work out the maximum biomass yield. For instance, Varma and Palsson used FBA to quantitatively anticipate the maximum cell growth rate cell density of wild-type _E. coli_ W3110 [[67](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B67-bioengineering-09-00473)]. In their study, they also used FBA to predict the temporal distribution of glucose as well as the concentration of by-products. Another important application of FBA focuses on the prediction of biomass production under different substrate conditions, which aims to improve medium formulation and/or medium feeding strategies [[63](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B63-bioengineering-09-00473)]. enriched_points.expanded_content: Innovative bioprocessing technologies enhance fermentation efficiency, enabling real-time monitoring and adaptive control to optimize microbial growth and product yield at industrial scales.

Cost-effective approaches, including investment in scalable infrastructure and efficient resource management, are crucial to reducing production costs and achieving competitive pricing in the fermentation industry.
Active collaboration with regulatory bodies ensures compliance, streamlining approval processes for novel fermentation products while addressing safety and environmental concerns effectively.
Implementing eco-friendly practices in fermentation processes minimizes waste, reduces carbon footprints, and promotes the use of renewable resources, aligning with global sustainability goals.
